definition: Use of organisms, cells or enzymes to make useful products and processes.
Direct manipulation of DNA to produce a desired trait, molecule or organism.
Large-scale growth and product recovery under sterile, controlled conditions.
A DNA carrier used to transfer a gene into a host cell.
A vector that can replicate in a host and carry inserted foreign DNA.
Origin of replication; the DNA sequence where replication begins.
A gene that helps identify transformed cells, often through antibiotic resistance.
A DNA sequence that reads the same in the 5' to 3' direction on both strands.
A DNA end with a short single-stranded overhang that can pair with a complementary overhang.
A DNA end with no single-stranded overhang.
Polymerase chain reaction; a method for amplifying a DNA segment through repeated thermal cycles.
Recovery of a desired DNA fragment from an agarose gel.
Uptake of foreign genetic material by a cell.
A host cell prepared to take up foreign DNA.
Loss of marker function when foreign DNA inserts into that marker gene.
Separation, purification, formulation and quality testing of a biosynthetic product.
Gene silencing caused by degradation or blocking of complementary mRNA.
A diagnostic method based on antigen-antibody interaction and a measurable signal.
Unfair or unauthorised use of bioresources or traditional knowledge.
Genetic Engineering Appraisal Committee, associated with biosafety evaluation of GM organisms in India.
